title: Fuel pump failure — in-tank design
description: 2004+ Sportsters use an in-tank EFI fuel pump. Same failure mode as Big Twins — running the tank low overheats the pump because fuel cools it. No prime whirr on key-on = pump or relay dead.
fix_procedure: 1. Key on — listen for 2-second prime whirr from tank. No sound = pump or relay. 2. Check fuel pump fuse and relay. 3. Check voltage at pump connector (under tank). 4. If no pump, replace. 5. Forum tip: the Sportster fuel pump assembly includes the filter/screen. Always replace the screen with the pump — a $10 screen is usually half-clogged on a dead pump. Delphi pump assemblies from Amazon are half the price of OEM and work fine.

title: Engine mount rubber isolator deterioration
description: The rubber engine mount isolators on 2004+ Sportsters deteriorate over time, allowing more vibration to transfer to the frame. The bike shakes more at idle and low RPM than when new. Not catastrophic but degrades ride quality.
fix_procedure: 1. Inspect engine mounts — look for cracked, soft, or oil-soaked rubber. 2. Replace all mount isolators as a set. 3. Torque to spec. 4. Forum tip: polyurethane mount replacements (Energy Suspension) last longer than rubber but transmit slightly more vibration. Depends on whether you want smoothness (rubber) or durability (poly). Most Sportster guys go poly because they're cheaper in the long run.
parts_needed: Engine mount isolator kit (rubber OEM or polyurethane)

title: ECM/EFI lean stumble — decel pop and off-idle hesitation
description: Same lean factory tune issue as Big Twins. Sportsters are particularly bad because the smaller displacement makes the lean condition more noticeable. Decel popping, off-idle hesitation, and hot running are the big complaints.
fix_procedure: 1. Install EFI tuner: FP3, Power Vision, or ThunderMax. 2. If stock exhaust, a canned map improves things significantly. 3. If aftermarket exhaust, tune is mandatory. 4. Forum tip: for Sportsters, the FP3 is the most popular because it's easy and Bluetooth. The cheapest option is a Dobeck TFI — it adds fuel without reprogramming the ECM. A Stage 1 tune (air cleaner + slip-ons + FP3) transforms a Sportster.
parts_needed: EFI tuner (FP3, Power Vision, or ThunderMax)

title: Stator connector melt — Sportster specific
description: 2004-2006 Sportsters are particularly prone to the stator connector melting due to the compact under-seat wiring layout. The connector gets hot, resistance increases, more heat, eventual melting and charging failure.
fix_procedure: 1. INSPECT NOW if you smell burning. 2. Check stator connector — look for melted/discolored plastic. 3. If melted, cut connector off and hard-wire with ring terminals and heat shrink. 4. Replace stator and regulator. 5. Forum tip: this is the same fire hazard as the Evo. Hard-wire it — never reuse a melted connector. Cycle Electric makes a Sportster-specific stator/regulator kit that eliminates the problem connector entirely.

title: Intake manifold leak — lean codes and surging
description: Same intake seal issue as all Harleys, but Sportster EFI models throw specific lean codes. P0131 (front O2 low voltage) and P1010 (intake leak detected) are the telltale signs.
fix_procedure: 1. Spray test at idle. 2. Replace both seals. 3. James Gasket JGI-27062-95 with Hylomar. 4. Forum tip: on Sportsters, the rear intake seal is harder to reach due to the frame backbone. Use a long reach tool. Same advice as always — do both seals, use new clamps, don't overtighten.

title: Rear brake caliper piston seizure
description: Sportster rear brake calipers can develop a seized piston from corrosion and brake fluid contamination. The rear brake drags, causing heat, pad wear, and reduced performance. More common in humid/wet climates or when brake fluid is not changed regularly.
fix_procedure: 1. Check: rear wheel should spin freely with brake released. If it drags, caliper is seized. 2. Remove caliper and push piston back — if it won't move or is rough, rebuild or replace. 3. Rebuild: clean piston and bore, replace seals and dust boot. 4. Flush entire brake system with fresh DOT 4. 5. Forum tip: flush brake fluid every 2 years regardless — DOT 4 absorbs moisture from the air. Corroded caliper pistons are always from old fluid. A caliper rebuild kit is $20 — cheaper than a new caliper ($200+).

title: Turn signal auto-cancel not working — TSSM issue
description: 2004+ Sportsters with the TSSM (Turn Signal/Security Module) can lose auto-cancel function for the turn signals. Signals stay on until manually cancelled. Usually a TSSM software glitch, sometimes a vehicle speed sensor issue.
causes: Vehicle speed sensor not sending signal
fix_procedure: 1. Check if speedometer is reading correctly — if speedo works, VSS is fine. 2. If speedo doesn't work, replace VSS on transmission. 3. If speedo works but auto-cancel doesn't, try disconnecting battery for 10 minutes to reset TSSM. 4. If still broken, TSSM may need replacement or reflash. 5. Forum tip: some guys just learn to manually cancel signals — it's annoying but not worth replacing the TSSM ($200+) for a convenience feature. Check the ground connection first — a bad ground causes all kinds of weird TSSM behavior.

title: Primary chain noise — shared oil makes it worse
description: Sportster primary chains are noisier than Big Twin primaries because the shared oil system means the primary chain runs in oil that's been through the engine and transmission. Sheared oil doesn't lubricate as well.
fix_procedure: 1. Change oil more frequently (every 2500 miles). 2. Check primary chain tension through derby cover. 3. Adjust or replace tensioner shoe if worn. 4. Forum tip: shorter oil change intervals are the single best thing for Sportster primary chain life. The chain eats oil quality — fresh oil = quiet chain. Also, some Sportster guys add 2oz of Lucas Heavy Duty Oil Stabilizer to the oil — it thickens the oil film on the chain.

title: Handlebar switch housing failure — intermittent controls
description: The handlebar switch housings (left for signals/horn/high-beam, right for start/kill) develop intermittent contact issues from vibration and weather exposure. Buttons work sometimes but not others.
fix_procedure: 1. Identify which switch is intermittent. 2. Remove switch housing and clean contacts with electrical contact cleaner. 3. Check wiring for breaks at the switch body — flex them while testing. 4. If contacts are pitted, replace switch housing. 5. Forum tip: before buying a new switch housing ($80-150), try spraying the switch with CRC QD Electronic Cleaner — it cleans contacts without disassembly. Works 60% of the time on intermittent Sportster switches. Also apply dielectric grease to the connectors afterward.

title: Fork seal leak — oil on fork tubes
description: Sportster fork seals leak from road debris nicking the fork tubes (chrome or hard-chrome) and tearing the seal lip. Oil weeps down the fork tubes and collects on the lower triple clamp or front brake caliper. A safety issue if oil gets on the brake rotor.
fix_procedure: 1. Check fork tubes for nicks or rust — run a fingernail down the tube surface. Any catch = the nick will tear a new seal. 2. If tubes are nicked, polish with 1500-grit sandpaper or replace tubes. 3. Replace fork seals and dust boots. 4. Refill with correct weight fork oil (check service manual — typically 10W or Type E). 5. Forum tip: before replacing seals, try a fork seal cleaning tool (Motion Pro seal driver or a cut-up film negative slid under the seal). Sometimes dirt gets under the seal lip and causes the leak — cleaning it out buys you another 10K miles. Also, fork boots (gaiters) prevent debris from reaching the seals in the first place.
